title: Make a button light an LED
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire a push button to one pin and a LED to another, upload the Arduino sketch, and watch the LED light the instant you press. Along the way, learn why an input pin needs a resistor to read reliably at all — the idea behind every button, switch, and sensor you'll wire from here on.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for Button and LED: a LED on GPIO 2 through a 220 ohm resistor to ground, and a push button on GPIO 13 held by 10 kilo-ohm resistors, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: LED
parts.explanation: A LED is a one-way light. It only lights when its longer leg (the +) faces the pin and its shorter leg (the −) faces ground. Too much current burns it out, so a LED always sits behind a resistor.

parts.name: 220 Ω resistor
parts.explanation: A resistor is a small component whose whole job is to limit how much current flows through a wire. Current is the flow of electricity through the circuit; a resistor throttles that flow down to a safe amount. Its strength is measured in ohms (Ω) — a larger number resists harder and lets less current through. A part like a LED wired straight to power pulls far more current than it can survive and burns out at once, so it sits in series with a resistor whose value is chosen to hold the current gentle enough to keep it alive. The coloured bands print the resistor's value, and it works the same either way round — a resistor has no positive or negative end.

parts.name: 2 × 10 kΩ resistors
parts.explanation: A pull resistor is a resistor put to a different job than limiting current: it ties an input pin to a known voltage. An input pin with nothing wired to it floats — it drifts between HIGH and LOW and reads at random. A 10 kΩ resistor holds the pin at a steady resting level. Its value is large on purpose: it pulls just firmly enough to set that resting level, yet gently enough that pressing the button — a near-zero-resistance path — easily overrules it and flips the pin the other way.

parts.name: Push button
parts.explanation: This push button has four pins in two connected pairs. Pressing it bridges the pairs and completes the circuit; releasing it breaks the connection again. It is the simplest way to hand the board a yes-or-no input.

parts.name: Jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: This pin switches the LED on and off.
Completes the LED's path back to zero volts.
The board reads this pin to feel each press.
Holds GPIO 13 HIGH until a press pulls it LOW.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical connection at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
Watch for LED polarity and the 220 Ω series resistor — the two most common first-circuit mistakes.
Teach the core idea once the circuit works- an input pin is high-impedance, so a floating pin reads random noise and the 10 kΩ pull-up gives it a steady resting level a press can override.
Explain the active-low reading- the pull-up holds GPIO 13 HIGH at rest, and a press to ground reads LOW, which is why the sketch checks for LOW.
Connect the two code paths- the Arduino sketch uses an external pull-up with plain INPUT, while the MicroPython version uses the chip's built-in pull-up via INPUT_PULLUP (Pin.PULL_UP).
If the LED never lights, check its direction, the resistor, the GPIO 2 wire, and the shared ground before changing code.
If the LED is always on, suspect the button wiring or its 10 kΩ resistor on GPIO 13.
For the challenge, have them remove the pull-up with power off to watch the pin float, then restore it — supervise so USB is unplugged during any rewiring.
Offer short explanations on request; do not dump theory before the learner has a working circuit.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Place the LED so its long leg (+) is on the GPIO 2 side and its short leg (−) heads toward ground.
Put the 220 Ω resistor in series between GPIO 2 and the LED's long leg.
Wire the push button so one side reaches GPIO 13 and the other reaches ground.
Add the 10 kΩ resistor that holds GPIO 13 steady until the button is pressed.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_02.1_ButtonAndLed.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Press the button and watch the LED.

arduino.explanation: Sets GPIO 13 as an input so the board can read the button.
True only while the button is held — a press pulls the pin LOW.
Lights the LED; the else branch turns it off again.

micropython.explanation: Reads GPIO 13 as an input with a built-in pull-up — the same job the 10 kΩ resistor does in hardware.
True when the pin reads LOW, which is the press.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: An input pin reads its voltage as HIGH or LOW; a pull resistor gives it a resting level so a press has something to flip.

notes.title: Why a press reads LOW
notes.body: The pull-up holds GPIO 13 at 3.3V, so at rest it reads HIGH. A press connects the pin to ground and it reads LOW, which is why the sketch checks for LOW. This backwards-feeling wiring is common in real circuits, so it's worth meeting early.

notes.title: The pull-up the chip already has
notes.body: This circuit wires its own 10 kΩ pull-up and reads the pin with plain INPUT. Every ESP32-S3 input pin also carries a pull-up resistor built into the chip; INPUT_PULLUP switches that one on so you can skip the external part. The MicroPython version does precisely this with Pin.PULL_UP.

expectedOutputExample: Press and hold the button — the LED lights immediately.
Let go — the LED goes dark.
It follows your finger with no delay for as long as the board has power.
test.successCriteria: This press is momentary — the LED only stays on while you hold it. Tomorrow's lamp will make it latch.

firstChecks: Check the LED's direction and the 220 Ω resistor, then the GPIO 2 wire.
The button or its 10 kΩ resistor is likely miswired on GPIO 13.
Re-check the shared ground, then that the sketch uploaded.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: With power off, remove the 10 kΩ resistor so GPIO 13 floats, run the sketch and watch the LED drift and react to your hand, then restore the resistor and watch the reading go rock steady.
